result: Fog clung to the harbor when I stepped outside this morning, a gauzy shawl draped over the cranes and moored ferries. The city felt paused, as if the sky had taken a slow breath and forgotten to exhale. I walked along the waterfront with a cup of scalding coffee, letting the steam mix with the low clouds, doubling the mist. The gulls, usually brazen, were subdued, their calls muffled by the damp air, their wings heavy with dew. Halfway to the fish market I paused to watch an elderly couple perform tai chi beside a stack of lobster traps. Each movement seemed to stitch the fog to the ground, graceful, deliberate, stubborn in the quiet. Nearby, a street musician coaxed gentle notes from a weather-beaten accordion, practicing for an audience that might never arrive. The melody drifted toward the water, wrapping itself around the shrouded hulls. Moments like these tempt me to believe time can be persuaded to slow, that the whir of deadlines and devices can be tuned down to a softer hum. Of course the spell will break; trucks will arrive, sirens will test their echoes, the day will demand answers. Still, it comforts me to know that a small harbor can host an unscripted symphony whenever the clouds decide to descend. Fog, after all, is simply water taking the scenic route between sea and sky. For a few stolen minutes, I was allowed to wander along that route, unhurried and entirely awake. I carried that hush homeward.

For a few stolen minutes, I was allowed to wander along that route, unhurried and entirely awake. I carried that hush homeward.
